speaker: interviewer
text: August 30, 1995, Edith Wertheim, maiden name Pewzner, interviewer, Deborah Lewin, Fort Lee, New Jersey, United States of America, in English. This is Deborah Lewin, D-E-B-O-R-A-H, interviewing Edith Wertheim in Fort Lee, New Jersey in English on August 30, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi. Let's begin by having you tell us your name and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Wertheim, W-E-R-T-H-E-I-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pewzner, P-E-W-Z-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 4, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what it was like growing up in Cracow before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not so wonderful, because I remember I start to go to school. My mother registered me in a Polish school. And I went to the school. We were only two Jewish girls between 20-something not Jewish. So they-- every day, I came home. I had spots from ink on, on my dress, on my, on my blouse, you know. And they were, they were terrible children, doing always something for spite. It wasn't good times, but all right. I was young. I, I was laughing it off in there, you know. But later on, got worse and worse because the anti-Semitism was terrible in schools, in, in our private life, in our social life. And it wasn't nice to grow up in  Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like within your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- I had a big family, wonderful family. My father died when I was six years old. And my sisters, older sisters, took care of me because I was a late baby. My mother was already 46 when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four brothers and with me, four sisters, so was a nice, big family. We grew up very nice together. It was a Jewish family, not very strict Jewish, but nice. We kept up the holidays. We are going to the synagogue. It was nice to grow up with a big family. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your, your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: My house-- we had an apartment on the third floor in a, not a Jewish neighborhood. It was in the middle of the city. It was most Catholic families. But I got along very well with my neighbors' children. We were playing together. And it was a very nice house. Was a nice-- not big, four rooms. So we had nice space. We had a maid, a Polish maid, my mother had. And that maid was helping to bring me up, because I was little. So with my sisters, they took care of me. [LAUGHS] It was a nice life, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share a room with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. I always slept with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of woman was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a Russian woman, a beautiful woman. She was beautiful. She was very energetic, very, very, very-- I have to tell you, she took care of all the children. She took care of the house. She cooked delicious. And she was a nice, religious woman, very fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was very close to them because I was the little one. So everybody was playing with me, [LAUGHS] like with a doll. You know, I got very older sisters and brothers, so you can imagine. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things did you like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I like to do? I read a lot. I watch television. I go to movies. The most, I love operas.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I grow up, my brother was a conductor in a symphony orchestra. My father was a violinist. My other brother was a piano player, a very good concert piano player. So I used to go every Sunday to a concert. We went to see my brother conducting the symphony orchestra. And that was my pleasure-- music, theater. I used to go to operas. I used to go to operettas, always something musical, musical. That was in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have favorite toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toys? I don't remember to having lots of toys. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends, we had, we had. Because from the neighborhood, not too many Jewish friends. But we used to have a spot in Cracow where we used to meet with the kids. And that was nice. So we we play around, you know. It was nice. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite Jewish holiday to celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Pesach. You know Pesach?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Pesach like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Delicious-- oh, it was beautiful. It was a family, family holiday. I didn't have a big family besides my closest, because my mother run away from Russia with my father with the, with the seven kids. So she left all the relatives there. So we did not have relatives in Poland. I never had an aunt. I never had a cousin. I never had no relatives. So I was only with my friends and with my family. But it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, Pesach. We used to go to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned anti-Semitism. Can you give some specific accounts of events that occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never-- nobody beat me up. Nobody never touched me. But just the school days were very, very, very terrible to go to school every day knowing that I have enemies around and they do something bad to me. Because we used to wear uniforms, you know, all the girls the same-- navy skirts and red, red blouses, you know. So every day, I, I used to come with spots. And I didn't have too much clothes. So it was not nice. [LAUGHS] But it, it's all right. I took everything, because I was always smiling. I was always happy. No matter what happened, I was always happy. So I came home with a spot. My mother said, all right, I try take it out like this. And that was-- but then my mother changed the schools. She went to the Jewish neighborhood. We had the Jewish neighborhood, the Jewish schools. So my mother registered me in a Jewish school, which was not so for me, because I had to walk, very far to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far?

speaker: interviewee
text: How far? Sometimes, I used to take a trolley car. When I didn't take a trolley car, I used to walk a half an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when I was seven, eight, nine. And I was growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked school. I was very good in school. I was very good in school. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a little girl, did you dream of the future, what it might be like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never, I never knew that something will happen that will change my life, you know. I thought, this is it. That's how I'm going to grow up and that's how it's going to be. And some day, maybe I'm going to get married like my sister, the oldest. And this is going to be my life. But then came the shock. And I never dreamt about good things, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no good dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1939-- what do you remember about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember. We changed our apartment. We didn't live anymore in the middle of the city. My mother moved. And we were living like a little bit outskirt out of Cracow. So it was a nice apartment. In '39 I was very much troubled, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I married a year before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married a, I married a nice man. And I didn't want to marry him, but my mother told me, you have to get married. This is it. And this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long story with that. But I married him. And when the war start, everybody, all the men, were running away from Cracow, you know. So everybody run away. My husband run away also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max. Max. He run away. And then when he run away, I was left with a little boy. I had a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo, Leo, after my father he was named. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my husband ran away to Russia. And he left me with my mother, my sisters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how I was with my mother at the time. That was very bad to take care of everything But I had the sister, the oldest sister. And she, she was very nice provide by her husband He was a nice man, my brother-in-law. He had a beautiful job, made a lot of money. So he used to help us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Abraham Sauber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was Helen Sauber. So she used to help out. And we were living. Everything is living, good living, bad living.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what specific ways did life change for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Jews, terrible. First, they didn't let into schools. They didn't want to take to schools us. They didn't-- the colleges were closed for Jews. On the street, they used to make like such a-- the, the college boys, the Catholic college boys, they used to run around in the city, beat up the Jewish boys. And it was a, it was a horror. It was horrible to live in these circumstances. You were afraid of your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, they came out with the star. And we had to wear a star, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were Jewish businesses affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. They closed up the businesses. The Jews were making like kind of black market, you know. It was very-- I can tell you the truth that I-- things like that, it's like somebody wiped out from my mind. I don't remember. Or I, or I wanted to forget. But I forgot. I, I can't come back with what happened to the, to the, to the businesses or to that. I can't remember. I know people lost a lot of businesses. They couldn't work. It was very hard. Life was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother moved, was she forced to move, or did she choose to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't forced. But she couldn't afford anymore to pay for the apartment so much. And so she moved away from it. It was in the middle of the city. It was like a luxurious apartment, not like that, but luxurious. So we moved a little worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were staying with your sister Helen and her husband, were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I never was staying with my sister. My sister was in-- she was living at the other apartment. And she used to help us out. We used to go to the-- I was working in a office. I was secretary also to a lawyer, two lawyers, a matter of fact. And I remember the names of the lawyers. Can you imagine that? [LAUGHS] Yeah, I remember both names of both lawyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One name was [NON-ENGLISH] and one name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they used to dictate me. I used to type what they were talking, because that time was not such a system like here, you know. That time, they used to walk and talk. And I had to type so quick, like they were talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both Jewish, oh, yes-- Jewish men, very fine, very good men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was their practice and your job ultimately affected as the occupation continued?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was affected so that we, we, we couldn't-- we went to ghetto. And that stopped the whole hullabaloo. We couldn't work in the city, because they didn't let us. So the jobs stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the events that led up to your moving to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The event what we moved to the ghetto-- it came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you say that? The Germans, they came out with every Jew has to move to the ghetto. How do we move? Where do we move, you know? We had to register this and that. I was taking care of my mother, because my mother was older person. She needed my help. So I was a help to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning when we supposed to go to the ghetto, I got the permission to stay in Cracow, you know, to go into the ghetto. Because nobody-- you know, some people were sent out from Cracow to small towns. So I got permission. I went with my mother. I took my baby with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that time, all are moved. My sisters were moving and my brothers. Everybody moved. The brother from Warsaw, he ran away to Russia. So he didn't-- was, he was not in Cracow. He ran away to Russia. My oldest brother, Herman, he died. He got sick, and he died. Then the younger brother, Zygmund, he ran away also to Russia. [NON-ENGLISH] also run away to Russia. So brothers, was nobody home from the brothers. And one sister went to a smaller town with her little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna [NON-ENGLISH]. And they killed the boy in that little town. They threw him from a window, and they kill him. They kill her, so I lost a sister already, one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the town. It was a small town not far from Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I lost that sister also. And so we were left in the ghetto, smaller family. It was my older sister with her husband, my mother, me, my baby, and the two daughters of my sister, Sabina and Marylia, [NON-ENGLISH]. We were living in one big room with a small kitchen. That was OK. Later on, as I remember, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was going on for a while, that living there in the ghetto. It was miserable. Then I-- my sister came. One day, she came over. She came over with the story that her daughter, the oldest daughter, Sabina, they wanted to send her out from Cracow to another city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's afraid, because this girl was 16 years old, was a beautiful girl. She was worried about her. How could she send her child away? So she came to us and to my mother to ask me, I should give my papers away to her daughter to save her daughter with my life, like they say, you know. But I didn't have another way, because she was taking care of my baby. You know, she was helping to dress him, to feed him. I didn't have money, didn't have another way to save it. So I sacrificed it. I said, all right. You take care of my baby, and for that, I let myself go to some other girl. So my mother found a girlfriend there. This girlfriend got two boys. And she was moving to Skala. She didn't have papers to stay also in ghetto. So my mother told her to take me with her and take care of me. I left the baby. And I went to Skala, where she was taking care of me there. What did she take care of me? She used to cook dinner, or she made breakfast. So she gave me some food, you know. Meantime, my sister used to send me some money to help me out. Look, I gave her my life like this. I, I really gave my life for her daughter. So she helped me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to, every few days, I used to dress as a peasant girl-- you know, the kerchief and, and barefoot, no shoes, shoes in the hand. And I used to walk to sneak into the ghetto. I wanted to see my boy, to see him, how is he doing, how he-- you know, he was growing up without a mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Leo at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- how old was I? 20 I think, or 21, something. It was in '39 or '40. I'm born in '16, so how was I? 23, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did one get papers that allowed one to stay in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to have a reason. My mother was sick, that said that sick. And as sick, she needs help. So they had allowed me to be her nurse, taking care of her. That's how I got the paper. And that paper, I gave it to my niece. And she was there taking care of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe one of the times when you snuck into the ghetto to see your baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very hard. A few times, they caught up with me on the road, the Germans. They took me to a police station. But somehow, I was always lucky. Somebody was standing up for me. Oh, let her go. Let her go, you know. So I was sneaking a lot of times. Once, I was going back, And it was already evening. Was not dark, but was evening. And I was going from the ghetto to Skala, you know, very tired, knocked out, you know. And my husband passed me by on a bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know him. No, I didn't know nothing. I saw young boys going on their bicycle. And he, he stopped. And he asked me. He said, where are you going? I said I am going from the city. I'm going back. Didn't know he's Jewish, not Jewish, on a bicycle. I said, I'm going from Cracow to Skala. He said jump in on the back seat, and I take you. So he took me to Skala on the bicycle. And that's how it starts, the romance, Romeo and Juliet. [LAUGHS] And then he tried to help me a lot. He tried to help me. Tried to help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back and hear some more about what it was like to sneak into the ghetto and visit with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: To sneak in the ghetto wasn't easy. It was not easy. But somehow, there was Jewish police at the entrance, you know, Jewish police. And I always-- you know, I, I always knew how to talk. So I always tell them, you know, I didn't sneak through a broken hole or through a basement. It was not Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto boundaried away from the rest of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- I don't know. It was in the end of the city. It was a section that was a Jewish neighborhood before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there walls or gates?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all around, all around closed up, sure, sure. Everything was closed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of guards were at the gates?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the gate's wall, sometimes I was seeing two Germans, then the Jewish police. Because the ghetto was in the hands of the Jewish police, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish police treat the Jewish prisoners of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? I don't want to talk about my brothers and sisters not nice. Sometimes they were terrible, terrible, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but all right. You could deal with them, no picnic, still deal with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't go too often, because wasn't so easy to go to ghetto. And so I didn't go too often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your son recognize you when you would come visit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, sure, he knew Mama, Mama. Sure. And then what I want say, then came a Judenrein. You know what's a Judenrein. They were throwing out the Jews from Skala. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a horrible time. So I had to go and try to sneak in back to the ghetto. I should be with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went into the ghetto. I went in, and I was staying with my mother. Meantime, they-- before that-- no, it was wrong. Before I sneak in back to the ghetto, they kill my mother. They took her out from the room and took her on the street, shot her. And they killed her. How did I know? Because Sam, that was my boyfriend that time, he used to travel. He used to sneak in some leather, you know, to sell. So that's how it was. So he came. And he told me that they killed my mother. That's how I got the news about my mother. Do you remember when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what time of year. It was not winter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember. That, that, that, that shock got to me. And I, I forgot all about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have--
